subject: Chemistry
solution:
P gives positive iodoform (has $CH_3CO-$) but negative Tollen's (no $-CHO$).
Q (after acid hydrolysis) gives both tests, meaning Q has both $CH_3CO-$ and $-CHO$.
This indicates P has an acetal protecting an aldehyde group.
Option (1): $CH_3-CO-CH_2-CH(OCH_3)_2$
Has $CH_3CO-$ → positive iodoform
Has acetal $-CH(OCH_3)_2$, no free aldehyde → negative Tollen's
Hydrolysis: $-CH(OCH_3)_2 \xrightarrow{H^+/H_2O} -CHO$
Q = $CH_3-CO-CH_2-CHO$ gives both iodoform and Tollen's test
Molecular formula: $C_6H_{12}O_3$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For Q ($C_4H_9Cl$) to be optically active, it must have a chiral center.
P ($C_4H_8$) = but-2-ene ($CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3$)
P + HCl → Q: $CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3 + HCl \rightarrow CH_3-CH(Cl)-CH_2-CH_3$ (2-chlorobutane, chiral at C-2)
Q + $NH_3$ → R: 2-chlorobutane + $NH_3 \rightarrow CH_3-CH(NH_2)-CH_2-CH_3$ (2-aminobutane)
R → diazotization → hydrolysis → S: 2-aminobutane $\xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCl}$ diazonium salt $\xrightarrow{H_2O}$ $CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2-CH_3$ (butan-2-ol)

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Q + $Br_2$/KOH → R is Hofmann bromamide degradation: Amide → Amine (with one less carbon).
R = $C_6H_7N$ = $C_6H_5NH_2$ = Aniline
For Q to give aniline, Q = Benzamide ($C_6H_5CONH_2$)
$C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/KOH} C_6H_5NH_2$
P + hot $NH_3$ → Q: Carboxylic acid + $NH_3$ → Amide
P = Benzoic acid ($C_6H_5COOH$)
$C_6H_5COOH \xrightarrow{NH_3, \Delta} C_6H_5CONH_2$
P = Benzoic acid, Q = Benzamide, R = Aniline

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Benzene $\xrightarrow{HNO_3/H_2SO_4}$ Nitrobenzene (P) $\xrightarrow{Sn/HCl}$ Aniline (Q) $\xrightarrow{(CH_3CO)_2O}$ Acetanilide (R)
R $\xrightarrow{HNO_3/H_2SO_4}$ p-Nitroacetanilide (S) $\xrightarrow{HCl/EtOH, \Delta}$ p-Nitroaniline (T)
T = p-Nitroaniline ($O_2N-C_6H_4-NH_2$), molecular formula $C_6H_6N_2O_2$
Molar mass = $6(12) + 6(1) + 2(14) + 2(16) = 72 + 6 + 28 + 32 = 138$ g/mol
% N = $\frac{28}{138} \times 100 = 20.29\%$
Nearest integer = 20

subject: Chemistry
solution:
A. Arginine and Tryptophan are essential amino acids - True
B. Histidine contains imidazole ring (heterocyclic ring with N) - Statement is False
C. Proline has a 5-membered pyrrolidine ring, not 6-membered - False
D. Glycine ($NH_2-CH_2-COOH$) has no chiral center (α-carbon has two H atoms) - True
E. Cysteine side chain is $-CH_2-SH$ (thiol). $MeS-CH_2-CH_2-$ is the side chain of Methionine, not Cysteine - False
Correct statements: A and D only

subject: Chemistry
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement I: Checking geometry of each species:
$SF_4$: 4 bp + 1 lp → see-saw
$NH_4^+$: 4 bp → tetrahedral
$[NiCl_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ (d⁸) with weak field $Cl^-$ → $sp^3$ → tetrahedral
$XeF_4$: 4 bp + 2 lp → square planar
$[PtCl_4]^{2-}$: $Pt^{2+}$ (d⁸) → $dsp^2$ → square planar
$SeF_4$: 4 bp + 1 lp → see-saw
$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ with strong field $CN^-$ → $dsp^2$ → square planar
Only 2 species are tetrahedral. Statement I is false.
Statement II: $NO_2$ (odd electron), $BeH_2$ (4e⁻), $BF_3$ (6e⁻), $AlCl_3$ (6e⁻) all have incomplete octet. Statement II is true.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
From the graph of G vs fraction of $N_2O_4$ dissociated:
At $x = 0$ (pure $N_2O_4$): $G = 0$
At equilibrium ($x \approx 0.4$): $G = -0.84$ kJ/mol (minimum)
At $x = 1$ (complete dissociation): $G = 5.40$ kJ/mol
Statement A: $\Delta G° = 5.40$ kJ/mol (positive), not $-5.40$ kJ/mol. False.
Statement B: Even with positive $\Delta G°$, equilibrium is established with partial dissociation. False.
Statement C: Reverse reaction reaches equilibrium, not completion. False.
Statement D: $\Delta G = G_{eq} - G_{N_2O_4} = -0.84 - 0 = -0.84$ kJ/mol. True.
Statement E: $\Delta G = G_{eq} - G_{NO_2} = -0.84 - 5.40 = -6.24$ kJ/mol. True.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Given: $A_1 = A_2$, $E_{a1} = E_{a2} + 20$ kJ/mol, T = 300 K, R = 8.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
Using Arrhenius equation: $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E_{a2}/RT}}{e^{-E_{a1}/RT}} = e^{(E_{a1} - E_{a2})/RT}$
$E_{a1} - E_{a2} = 20$ kJ/mol = 20000 J/mol
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{20000}{8.3 \times 300}} = e^{\frac{20000}{2490}}$
$\ln\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{20000}{2490} = 8.03 \approx 8$

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
solution:
A. F > P > S > B (IE₁): F has highest IE₁, P > S due to half-filled $3p^3$ stability, B has lowest. Correct
B. Cl > F > S > P (EA): Cl has highest EA (F smaller, more repulsion), S > P (half-filled p-orbital). Correct
C. K > Al > Mg > B (Metallic character): Incorrect. Mg (Group 2) is more metallic than Al (Group 13) in same period. Correct order: K > Mg > Al > B
D. $K_2O > Na_2O > MgO > Al_2O_3$ (Basic character): Follows electropositive nature. $Al_2O_3$ is amphoteric. Correct
Correct statements: A, B and D only.

subject: Chemistry
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I: Unpaired electrons in each complex:
$[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$: $Cu^{2+}$ is $d^9$ → 1 unpaired
$[Ni(en)_3]^{2+}$: $Ni^{2+}$ is $d^8$, octahedral → 2 unpaired
$[Ni(NH_3)_6]^{2+}$: $Ni^{2+}$ is $d^8$ → 2 unpaired
$[Mn(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$: $Mn^{2+}$ is $d^5$, weak field → high spin → 5 unpaired
$[Mn(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$ has maximum. Statement I is true.
Statement II: $[NiCl_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ ($d^8$), tetrahedral → paramagnetic
$[Ni(CO)_4]$: $Ni^0$ ($d^{10}$) → diamagnetic
$[Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}$: $Ni^{2+}$ ($d^8$), square planar → diamagnetic
Only pair $\{[Ni(CO)_4], [Ni(CN)_4]^{2-}\}$ has both diamagnetic. Count = 1, not 2. Statement II is false.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$MnO_4^{2-}$ (manganate) has Mn in +6 oxidation state.
In acidic medium, it undergoes disproportionation where Mn is simultaneously oxidized and reduced:
Oxidation: $+6 \rightarrow +7$ (forms $MnO_4^-$, permanganate)
Reduction: $+6 \rightarrow +4$ (forms $MnO_2$)
Reaction: $3MnO_4^{2-} + 4H^+ \rightarrow 2MnO_4^- + MnO_2 + 2H_2O$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Reaction 1: $NaCl + K_2Cr_2O_7 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow CrO_2Cl_2$ (A, chromyl chloride)
Reaction 2: $CrO_2Cl_2 + 4NaOH \rightarrow Na_2CrO_4 + 2NaCl + 2H_2O$, B = $Na_2CrO_4$
Reaction 3: $Na_2CrO_4 + H_2SO_4 + H_2O_2 \rightarrow CrO_5 + Na_2SO_4 + H_2O$
C = $CrO_5$ or $CrO(O_2)_2$ (chromium peroxide, blue)
Structure: one oxo group and two peroxide ($O_2^{2-}$) units
X = number of $O_2^{2-}$ units = 2
Y = total oxygen atoms = 5
Z = oxidation state of Cr: $O^{2-}(-2) + 2 \times O_2^{2-}(-4) = -6$, so Cr = +6
$X + Y + Z = 2 + 5 + 6 = 13$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Given: pH = 5, so $[H^+] = 10^{-5}$ M; G = $4 \times 10^{-5}$ S; A = 1 cm²; l = 15 cm
Cell constant = l/A = 15 cm⁻¹
Specific conductivity: $\kappa = G \times (l/A) = 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 15 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ S/cm
For weak acid HX: $[H^+] = C\alpha = 10^{-5}$ M
Using $\kappa = \frac{C\alpha \times \Lambda°_m}{1000}$
$\Lambda°_m = \frac{1000 \times \kappa}{C\alpha} = \frac{1000 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-5}} = 6 \times 10^4$ S cm² mol⁻¹
Converting: $\Lambda°_m = 6 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = 6$ S m² mol⁻¹

subject: Chemistry
solution:
In Carius method, sulfur is converted to $BaSO_4$. Each mole of $BaSO_4$ contains 1 mole of S.
Mass of S = $\frac{32}{233} \times 1.2 = 0.1648$ g
Percentage of S = $\frac{0.1648}{0.75} \times 100 = 21.97\%$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
A. Propanal ($CH_3CH_2CHO$) and propanone ($CH_3COCH_3$): Same formula $C_3H_6O$, different functional groups (aldehyde vs ketone) → Functional isomers. True
B. Ethoxyethane ($C_2H_5OC_2H_5$) and methoxypropane ($CH_3OC_3H_7$): Same ether group, different alkyl groups → Metamers. True
C. But-2-ene ($CH_3CH=CHCH_3$): No chiral carbon, shows geometrical isomerism (cis-trans), not optical. False
D. But-1-ene and but-2-ene: Both are alkenes, differ in position of double bond → Position isomers, not functional isomers. False
E. Pentane and 2,2-dimethylpropane: Both $C_5H_{12}$, different carbon skeleton → Chain isomers. True
Correct: A, B and E only.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
To determine the stability of resonating structures, we follow these rules:
1. Structures with more covalent bonds are generally more stable.
2. Structures where all atoms have complete octets are more stable.
3. Separation of opposite charges decreases stability, while like charges on adjacent atoms highly destabilize the structure.
4. Negative charge on a more electronegative atom and positive charge on a more electropositive atom is preferred.
In structure (4), there are two positive charges ($+$) located on adjacent atoms (the carbon atom and the nitrogen atom of the nitro group).
Electrostatic repulsion between like charges on adjacent atoms makes the structure highly unstable.
In structures (1), (2), and (3), like charges are not on adjacent atoms.
Therefore, structure (4) is the least stable.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Reaction 1: $PbCl_2 + K_2CrO_4 \rightarrow PbCrO_4 + 2KCl$
A = $PbCrO_4$ (lead chromate)
Reaction 2: $PbCrO_4 + 4NaOH \rightarrow Na_2[Pb(OH)_4] + Na_2CrO_4$
B = $Na_2[Pb(OH)_4]$ (sodium tetrahydroxoplumbate)
Reaction 3: $PbSO_4 + 4CH_3COONH_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4 + (NH_4)_2[Pb(CH_3COO)_4]$
X = $(NH_4)_2[Pb(CH_3COO)_4]$ (ammonium tetraacetatoplumbate)

subject: Chemistry
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I: Checking amphoteric nature of oxide pairs:
$[SiO_2, CO_2]$: Both acidic (non-metal oxides)
$[SnO, SnO_2]$: Both amphoteric
$[PbO, PbO_2]$: $PbO$ is amphoteric but $PbO_2$ is predominantly acidic/oxidizing
$[GeO, GeO_2]$: Both amphoteric (Ge is metalloid)
2 pairs have both oxides amphoteric. Statement I is true.
Statement II: $BF_3$ has B with 6 valence electrons (electron deficient), acts as Lewis acid, forms $BF_3 \cdot NH_3$ adduct, has $sp^2$ hybridization giving trigonal planar geometry. Statement II is true.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Using $\Delta T_b = K_b \times \frac{w}{M} \times \frac{1000}{W}$
For PQ: $1.176 = 5 \times \frac{1}{M_{PQ}} \times \frac{1000}{50} = \frac{100}{M_{PQ}}$
$M_{PQ} = \frac{100}{1.176} = 85$ g/mol
For $PQ_2$: $0.689 = \frac{100}{M_{PQ_2}}$
$M_{PQ_2} = \frac{100}{0.689} = 145$ g/mol
$P + Q = 85$ ... (i)
$P + 2Q = 145$ ... (ii)
Subtracting: $Q = 60$ g/mol
From (i): $P = 85 - 60 = 25$ g/mol

subject: Chemistry
options: The limiting reagent is calcium metal.
solution:
Reaction: $Ca + 2HCl \rightarrow CaCl_2 + H_2$
Moles of Ca = $\frac{14.0}{40} = 0.35$ mol. Since HCl is in excess, Ca is limiting.
Option (1): $H_2$ evolved = 0.35 mol
Option (2): Volume of $H_2$ at STP = $0.35 \times 22.4 = 7.84$ L
Option (3): Ca is limiting reagent
Option (4): Mass of $CaCl_2 = 0.35 \times 111 = 38.85$ g, not 33.3 g
Statement (4) is incorrect.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
KOH absorbs $CO_2$. Volume of $CO_2$ produced = $224 - 64 = 160$ mL
Unreacted $O_2 = 64$ mL, so $O_2$ consumed = $264 - 64 = 200$ mL
For hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$: $\frac{CO_2}{\text{Hydrocarbon}} = \frac{160}{80} = 2 \Rightarrow x = 2$
$O_2$ consumed per mole = $x + \frac{y}{4} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5$
$2 + \frac{y}{4} = 2.5 \Rightarrow y = 2$
Hydrocarbon is $C_2H_2$

subject: Chemistry
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement I: Electric discharge through $H_2$ causes dissociation into excited H atoms which emit discrete frequencies (line spectrum). This is true.
Statement II: Using $ u = RcZ^2\left( rac{1}{n_1^2} - rac{1}{n_2^2} ight)$
For $He^+$ (Z=2), second Balmer line ($n_1=2, n_2=4$): $ u = 4Rc\left( rac{1}{4} - rac{1}{16} ight) = rac{3Rc}{4}$
For H (Z=1), first Lyman line ($n_1=1, n_2=2$): $ u = Rc\left(1 - rac{1}{4} ight) = rac{3Rc}{4}$
Both frequencies are equal. Statement II is true.
Both statements are true.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
In a pressure-volume ($P-V$) graph, the magnitude of work done is represented by the area under the curve bounded by the volume axis.
Let's analyze the area under the curve for each graph:
Graph (1) represents an expansion from $V = 22.4$ L to $V = 44.8$ L. The area is the integral $\int_{22.4}^{44.8} P dV$, which covers a significant region under the curve.
Graph (2) represents a cyclic process. The net work done is the area enclosed by the loop. This area is much smaller than the total area under the expansion curve in graph (1).
Graph (3) represents an isochoric process (constant volume $V = 22.4$ L). Since $dV = 0$, the work done $W = \int P dV = 0$.
Graph (4) also represents a cyclic process. Similar to graph (2), the net work done is the area enclosed by the loop, which is smaller than the area under the expansion curve in graph (1).
Comparing all cases, the expansion process in graph (1) covers the maximum area under the $P-V$ curve, thus representing the maximum work done.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Reaction: $A_2(g) + B_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2AB(g)$
$\Delta G° = -2.303RT \log K = -2.303 \times 8.3 \times 500 \times 2.2 = -21026$ J/mol = $-21.03$ kJ/mol
$\Delta S° = 2(222) - [146 + 280] = 444 - 426 = 18$ J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
$\Delta H° = 2(32) - [6 + x] = (58 - x)$ kJ/mol
Using $\Delta G° = \Delta H° - T\Delta S°$:
$-21.03 = (58 - x) - (500 \times 0.018)$
$-21.03 = 58 - x - 9 = 49 - x$
$x = 49 + 21.03 = 70.03 \approx 70$ kJ/mol

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For equal roots in $f(x)m^2 - 2f'(x)m + f''(x) = 0$, discriminant = 0:
$[f'(x)]^2 = f(x)f''(x)$
This gives $\frac{f''(x)}{f'(x)} = \frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$
Integrating: $f'(x) = kf(x)$, so $f(x) = Ae^{kx}$
Using $f(0) = 1$ and $f'(0) = 2$: $A = 1$, $k = 2$
$f(x) = e^{2x}$
For $g(x) = f(\ln x - x)$:
$g'(x) = 2e^{2(\ln x - x)} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{x} - 1\right)$
$g'(x) > 0$ when $\frac{1-x}{x} > 0$, i.e., $0 < x < 1$
$(\alpha, \beta) = (0, 1)$
$\alpha + \beta = 1$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Ellipse: $\frac{x^2}{4} + y^2 = 1$ with $a = 2$, $b = 1$. Area $= \pi ab = 2\pi$.
Curves $y = |x| - 1$ and $y = 1 - |x|$ intersect at $x = \pm 1$, forming a rhombus with vertices $(0, 1)$, $(1, 0)$, $(0, -1)$, $(-1, 0)$.
Vertices $(0, \pm 1)$ lie on ellipse; $(\pm 1, 0)$ are inside ellipse.
Area of rhombus $= \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2 = 2$.
Required area $= 2\pi - 2 = 2(\pi - 1)$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
From $\vec{c} \times \vec{a} = \vec{a} \times \vec{d}$ where $\vec{a} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 4\hat{k}$, we get $(\vec{c} + \vec{d}) \times \vec{a} = 0$.
So $\vec{c} + \vec{d} = t\vec{a}$. Given $|\vec{c} + \vec{d}| = \sqrt{29}$: $|t| \cdot \sqrt{29} = \sqrt{29}$, so $t = \pm 1$.
$(\vec{c} + \vec{d}) \cdot (-7\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) = \pm(-14 + 6 + 12) = \pm 4$.
$\lambda_1 = 4$, $\lambda_2 = -4$.
For circle: Coeff of $x^2$ = Coeff of $y^2$: $K^2 = 3K - 2 \Rightarrow K = 1, 2$.
Coeff of $xy = 0$: $K^2 - 5K + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow K = 1, 4$.
Common value: $K = 1$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Circle: $(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2 = 16$, center O = (2, 3), radius $r = 4$.
For tangents from external point P touching at A and B with $\angle AOB = \pi/3$:
In quadrilateral OAPB: $\angle APB = \pi - \pi/3 = 2\pi/3$, so $\angle OPA = \pi/3$.
In right triangle OAP: $\sin(\pi/3) = \frac{4}{OP}$.
$OP = \frac{8}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{8\sqrt{3}}{3}$.
Locus: $(x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2 = \frac{64}{3}$.
$3(x^2 + y^2) - 12x - 18y - 25 = 0$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given $x^2 + x + 1 = 0$, so $x = \omega$ (primitive cube root of unity) with $x^3 = 1$.
From the equation: $x + \frac{1}{x} = -1$.
Since $x^3 = 1$, value of $x^n + x^{-n}$ has period 3:
$n \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$: $x^n + x^{-n} = 2$
$n \equiv 1, 2 \pmod{3}$: $x^n + x^{-n} = -1$
Fourth powers: $(2)^4 = 16$, $(-1)^4 = 1$.
For $n = 1$ to $25$: 8 multiples of 3 contribute $8 \times 16 = 128$, remaining 17 terms contribute $17 \times 1 = 17$.
Total $= 128 + 17 = 145$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Split: $\int_{-\pi/6}^{\pi/6} \frac{\pi}{1 - \sin(|x| + \pi/6)} dx + \int_{-\pi/6}^{\pi/6} \frac{4x^{11}}{1 - \sin(|x| + \pi/6)} dx$.
Second integral = 0 (odd function over symmetric interval).
First integral: $2\pi \int_{0}^{\pi/6} \frac{1}{1 - \sin(x + \pi/6)} dx$.
Let $u = x + \pi/6$: $= 2\pi \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3} \frac{1}{1 - \sin u} du$.
$\frac{1}{1-\sin u} = \sec^2 u + \sec u \tan u$.
$= 2\pi [\tan u + \sec u]_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3} = 2\pi[(\sqrt{3}+2) - \sqrt{3}] = 4\pi$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Using $\sin 3x + \sin x = 2\sin 2x \cos x$:
$\sin 3x + \sin 2x + \sin x = \sin 2x(2\cos x + 1) = 2\sin x \cos x(2\cos x + 1)$
Zeros in $[0, \pi]$: $x = 0, \pi/2, 2\pi/3, \pi$
Expression is positive on $(0, \pi/2)$, negative on $(\pi/2, 2\pi/3)$, positive on $(2\pi/3, \pi)$
Let $F(x) = -\frac{\cos 3x}{3} - \frac{\cos 2x}{2} - \cos x$
$F(0) = -\frac{11}{6}$, $F(\pi/2) = \frac{1}{2}$, $F(2\pi/3) = \frac{5}{12}$, $F(\pi) = \frac{5}{6}$
$\int_0^{\pi/2} = \frac{7}{3}$, $\left|\int_{\pi/2}^{2\pi/3}\right| = \frac{1}{12}$, $\int_{2\pi/3}^{\pi} = \frac{5}{12}$
Total $= \frac{7}{3} + \frac{1}{12} + \frac{5}{12} = \frac{17}{6}$
$6 \times \frac{17}{6} = 17$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Rewrite as $\frac{dy}{dx} + \frac{y}{1+x^2} = \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2}$.
IF $= e^{\tan^{-1}x}$.
$y \cdot e^{\tan^{-1}x} = \int \frac{\tan^{-1}x}{1+x^2} \cdot e^{\tan^{-1}x} dx$.
Let $t = \tan^{-1}x$: $= \int te^t dt = e^t(t-1) + C$.
$y = \tan^{-1}x - 1 + Ce^{-\tan^{-1}x}$.
Using $y(0) = 1$: $C = 2$.
$y(1) = \frac{\pi}{4} - 1 + \frac{2}{e^{\pi/4}}$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For ellipse $\frac{x^2}{36} + \frac{y^2}{16} = 1$: $a = 6$, $b = 4$, $c = \sqrt{36-16} = 2\sqrt{5}$.
Foci of ellipse: $(\pm 2\sqrt{5}, 0)$.
For hyperbola with eccentricity $e = 5$ and foci at $(\pm 2\sqrt{5}, 0)$:
$e = \frac{C}{A} \Rightarrow A = \frac{2\sqrt{5}}{5}$.
$C^2 = A^2 + B^2 \Rightarrow 20 = \frac{4}{5} + B^2 \Rightarrow B^2 = \frac{96}{5}$.
Latus rectum $= \frac{2B^2}{A} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{96}{5}}{\frac{2\sqrt{5}}{5}} = \frac{96}{\sqrt{5}}$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For $\cos^{-1}\left(\frac{2x-5}{11-3x}\right)$: $-1 \leq \frac{2x-5}{11-3x} \leq 1$ gives $x \leq \frac{16}{5}$ or $x > 6$.
For $\sin^{-1}(2x^2 - 3x + 1)$: $-1 \leq 2x^2 - 3x + 1 \leq 1$.
Lower bound always satisfied (discriminant $< 0$).
Upper bound gives $x(2x-3) \leq 0$, so $0 \leq x \leq \frac{3}{2}$.
Intersection of both conditions: $[0, \frac{3}{2}]$.
Thus $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \frac{3}{2}$.
$\alpha + 2\beta = 0 + 3 = 3$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $T = \lim_{x \to 1} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2)}{f(3)} \right)^{\dfrac{18}{(x-1)^2}}$ ; $1^{\infty}$ form
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{18}{(x-1)^2} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2) - f(3)}{f(3)} \right)}$
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{18}{(x-1)^2} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2) - f(3)}{18} \right)}$
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \left( \dfrac{f(x+2) - f(3)}{(x-1)^2} \right)}$ $\dfrac{0}{0}$ form, apply L'pital
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{f'(x+2)}{2(x-1)}}$ ; $\dfrac{0}{0}$ form, apply L'pital
$\Rightarrow T = e^{\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{f''(x+2)}{2}}$ $= e^{\dfrac{4}{2}} = e^2$
$\Rightarrow \log_e(T) = 2$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
From $A^2 - 4A + 2I = O$: characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 2 = 0$.
For $A$: $\alpha + 2 = 4 \Rightarrow \alpha = 2$.
From $B^2 - 3B + I = O$: characteristic equation is $\lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 1 = 0$.
For $B$: $\beta + 1 = 3 \Rightarrow \beta = 2$.
$A^3 = 14A - 8I$, $B^3 = 8B - 3I$.
$A^3 - B^3 = 14A - 8B - 5I = \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 20 \\ 6 & 7 \end{bmatrix}$.
$\det(A^3 - B^3) = 105 - 120 = -15$.
For 2×2 matrix: $\det(\text{adj}(M)) = \det(M)$.
$(\det(\text{adj}(A^3 - B^3)))^2 = 225$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Parabola $x^2 = 4y$, vertex O = (0, 0), Q = $(2t, t^2)$ on parabola.
P divides OQ in ratio 2:3: $P = \left(\frac{4t}{5}, \frac{2t^2}{5}\right)$.
Eliminating $t$: $x^2 = \frac{8y}{5}$ (conic C with $a = \frac{2}{5}$).
For chord with midpoint $(h, k)$: $hx - 2ay = h^2 - 2ak$.
At $(1, 2)$: $x - \frac{4y}{5} = 1 - \frac{8}{5} = -\frac{3}{5}$.
$5x - 4y + 3 = 0$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For strictly increasing $f$, if we choose elements $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_6$, then $f(i) = a_i$.
Since $f(i) \geq i$ always, condition $f(i) \neq i$ means $f(i) \geq i + 1$ for all $i$.
Let $h(i) = f(i) - i$. Then $h(i) \geq 1$, $h(6) \leq 3$, and $h$ is non-decreasing.
So $h(i) \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ for all $i$.
Number of non-decreasing sequences of length 6 from $\{1, 2, 3\} = \binom{8}{2} = 28$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For $6^m + 9^n \equiv 0 \pmod{5}$:
$6 \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$ and $9 \equiv -1 \pmod{5}$
So $6^m + 9^n \equiv 1 + (-1)^n \pmod{5}$
This equals 0 only when n is odd.
Number of odd n in {1,...,50} = 25, and m can be any of 50 values.
$p = 50 \times 25 = 1250$
For $m + n$ = square of prime, possible values are 4, 9, 25, 49.
$m + n = 4$: 3 pairs
$m + n = 9$: 8 pairs
$m + n = 25$: 24 pairs
$m + n = 49$: 48 pairs
$q = 3 + 8 + 24 + 48 = 83$
$p + q = 1250 + 83 = 1333$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $t = |x-1|$ where $t \geq 0$.
Equation becomes $t^2 - 5t + 6 = 0$.
$(t-2)(t-3) = 0$ gives $t = 2$ or $t = 3$.
For $|x-1| = 2$: $x = 3$ or $x = -1$.
For $|x-1| = 3$: $x = 4$ or $x = -2$.
Sum of roots $= 3 + (-1) + 4 + (-2) = 4$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let first term be $a$ and common ratio $r > 1$.
From $a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdot a_4 = 64$: $(ar)(ar^2)(ar^3) = a^3r^6 = 64 \Rightarrow ar^2 = 4$.
From $a_1 + a_3 + a_5 = \frac{813}{7}$: $a(1 + r^2 + r^4) = \frac{813}{7}$.
Substituting $a = \frac{4}{r^2}$: $4\left(\frac{1}{r^2} + 1 + r^2\right) = \frac{813}{7}$.
Let $u = r^2$: $28u^2 - 785u + 28 = 0$ gives $u = 28$ (since $r > 1$).
$a_3 + a_5 + a_7 = ar^2(1 + r^2 + r^4) = 4(1 + 28 + 784) = 3252$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $b_n = a_n - \frac{2}{n^2}$.
$b_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}a_n + \frac{n^2-2n-1}{n^2(n+1)^2} - \frac{2}{(n+1)^2}$.
Simplifying: $b_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2}b_n + \frac{(n+1)^2 + n^2 - 2n - 1 - 2n^2}{n^2(n+1)^2} = \frac{1}{2}b_n$.
So $b_n$ is GP with ratio $\frac{1}{2}$.
$b_1 = 1 - 2 = -1$, so $b_n = -\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1}$.
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n = -\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{2}} = -2$.
$\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_n - \frac{2}{n^2}\right)\right| = 2$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For a set with $n = 4$ elements, reflexive condition requires all diagonal pairs $(a,a), (b,b), (c,c), (d,d)$ to be included.
For symmetric relations, non-diagonal pairs come in symmetric pairs. Number of such pairs = $\binom{4}{2} = 6$.
For each pair, we either include both elements or exclude both → 2 choices each.
Total reflexive and symmetric relations = $2^6 = 64$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Mean = 8 gives $x + y = 14$.
Variance = 16 gives $(x-8)^2 + (y-8)^2 = 4$.
Solving: $x = 8$, $y = 6$ (since $x > y$).
Set becomes $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 5\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.
Total ways to choose 2 numbers = $\binom{6}{2} = 15$.
Favorable cases (smaller $< 4$): smaller = 1 gives 5 pairs, smaller = 2 gives 4 pairs, smaller = 3 gives 3 pairs.
Total favorable = 12.
Probability $= \frac{12}{15} = \frac{4}{5}$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Line: $\vec{r} = (-1, 3, 1) + \lambda(2, 3, -1)$, Point P = (5, 4, 2).
Any point on line: $Q = (-1 + 2\lambda, 3 + 3\lambda, 1 - \lambda)$.
$\vec{PQ} = (-6 + 2\lambda, -1 + 3\lambda, -1 - \lambda)$.
For perpendicular: $\vec{PQ} \cdot (2, 3, -1) = 0$ gives $14\lambda - 14 = 0$, so $\lambda = 1$.
Foot $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = (1, 6, 0)$.
Projection of $(1, 6, 0)$ on $(6, 2, 3)$: $\frac{|6 + 12 + 0|}{\sqrt{49}} = \frac{18}{7}$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\operatorname{cosec} 10° - \sqrt{3} \sec 10° = \frac{\cos 10° - \sqrt{3} \sin 10°}{\sin 10° \cos 10°}$.
Numerator: $\cos 10° - \sqrt{3} \sin 10° = 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\cos 10° - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin 10°\right) = 2\cos 70° = 2\sin 20°$.
Denominator: $\sin 10° \cos 10° = \frac{1}{2}\sin 20°$.
Result $= \frac{2\sin 20°}{\frac{1}{2}\sin 20°} = 4$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $\vec{c} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$.
From $\vec{a} \times \vec{c} = \vec{b}$: $z - y = 4$, $z + 2x = 7$, $y + 2x = 3$.
From $\vec{c} \cdot (\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}) = 4$: $x + y + z = 4$.
Solving: $y = -1$, $z = 3$, $x = 2$.
$\vec{c} = 2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$.
$\vec{a} + \vec{c} = (-1+2)\hat{i} + (2-1)\hat{j} + (2+3)\hat{k} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + 5\hat{k}$.
$|\vec{a} + \vec{c}|^2 = 1 + 1 + 25 = 27$.

subject: Physics
solution:
At closest approach, KE converts to PE:
$KE = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \times \frac{(2e)(Ze)}{r_{min}}$
$r_{min} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.7 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$= \frac{9 \times 2 \times 79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{7.7} \times 10^{3}$
$= \frac{2275.2}{7.7} \times 10^{-16} = 2.95 \times 10^{-14}$ m

subject: Physics
solution:
Original capacitance: $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$
After inserting dielectric of thickness $\frac{d}{3}$, system becomes two capacitors in series.
Vacuum region: $C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2d/3} = \frac{3C}{2}$
Dielectric region: $C_2 = \frac{K\varepsilon_0 A}{d/3} = 3KC$
Equivalent capacitance: $\frac{1}{C_{new}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} = \frac{2}{3C} + \frac{1}{3KC} = \frac{2K+1}{3KC}$
$C_{new} = \frac{3KC}{2K+1}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Wheatstone bridge with P = 1Ω, Q = 2Ω, R = 2Ω, S = 4Ω
$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S} = \frac{1}{2}$ → Bridge is balanced, no current through middle 1Ω.
$R_{AB}$: Upper (3Ω) and Lower (6Ω) in parallel = $\frac{3 \times 6}{9} = 2Ω$
Total resistance = 1 + 2 = 3Ω
Current $I = \frac{9}{3} = 3$ A
Power between A and B: $P = I^2 R_{AB} = 9 \times 2 = 18$ W
Heat in 1 min = $18 \times 60 = 1080$ J

subject: Physics
solution:
Light contains two frequencies: $\omega_1 = 3 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 12 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s
For maximum KE, use higher frequency $\omega_2$.
Energy: $E_2 = \frac{h\omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 12 \times 10^{15}}{2\pi}$
$= \frac{79.2 \times 10^{-19}}{6.28} = 12.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J $= 7.9$ eV
$KE_{max} = E_2 - \phi = 7.9 - 2.8 = 5.1$ eV

subject: Physics
solution:
Flux: $\phi = BA = \sin(100t)$ Wb
Induced EMF: $\varepsilon = -\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -100\cos(100t)$ V
Induced current: $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = -\cos(100t)$ A
Power dissipated: $P = I^2R = 100\cos^2(100t)$ W
Average power: $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 = 50$ W
Time period: $T = \frac{2\pi}{100}$ s
Energy in one period: $E = P_{avg} \times T = 50 \times \frac{2\pi}{100} = \pi$ J

subject: Physics
solution:
Given: $l = 1$ m, $B = 0.10$ T, $R = 2$ Ω, $v = 1.5$ m/s
Motional EMF: $\varepsilon = Blv = 0.10 \times 1 \times 1.5 = 0.15$ V
Current: $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = \frac{0.15}{2} = 0.075$ A
Magnetic force on rod: $F = BIl = 0.10 \times 0.075 \times 1 = 7.5 \times 10^{-3}$ N
For constant velocity, applied force equals magnetic force.

subject: Physics
solution:
Given $E_y = 69 \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ V/m
Wave propagates along +x (form $kx - \omega t$), $\vec{E}$ is along y.
For EM wave: $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ = direction of propagation
$\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$, so $\vec{B}$ is along z-direction.
$B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c} = \frac{69}{3 \times 10^8} = 2.3 \times 10^{-7}$ T
E and B are in phase for plane EM wave.
$B_z = 2.3 \times 10^{-7} \sin[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t]$ T

subject: Physics
solution:
Distance from origin to A: $r_A = \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2 + 2^2} = 6$ m
Distance from origin to B: $r_B = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = 3$ m
Work done: $W = q_2(V_B - V_A) = q_2 \times \frac{q_1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r_B} - \frac{1}{r_A}\right)$
$= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-8} \times \left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{6}\right)$
$= 18 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1}{6} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ J

subject: Physics
solution:
Total energy of satellite in orbit: $E = -\frac{GMm}{2r} = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2r}$
$E_1 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(1.5R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{3}$
$E_2 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(3R_E)} = -\frac{gR_E m}{6}$
Energy supplied $= E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{gR_E m}{6} + \frac{gR_E m}{3} = \frac{gR_E m}{6}$
$= \frac{10 \times 6 \times 10^6 \times 100}{6} = 10^9$ J $= 1000 \times 10^6$ J
$\alpha = 1000$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given $\vec{F} = (4t^3\hat{i} - 3t\hat{j})$ N, m = 4 kg
$\vec{a} = \frac{\vec{F}}{m} = t^3\hat{i} - \frac{3t}{4}\hat{j}$
Integrating with $\vec{v}(0) = 0$:
$\vec{v} = \frac{t^4}{4}\hat{i} - \frac{3t^2}{8}\hat{j}$
At $t = 2$: $\vec{v} = \frac{16}{4}\hat{i} - \frac{12}{8}\hat{j} = 4\hat{i} - \frac{3}{2}\hat{j}$
Integrating with $\vec{r}(0) = 0$:
$\vec{r} = \frac{t^5}{20}\hat{i} - \frac{t^3}{8}\hat{j}$
At $t = 2$: $\vec{r} = \frac{32}{20}\hat{i} - \frac{8}{8}\hat{j} = \frac{8}{5}\hat{i} - \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Inside a solenoid, magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is uniform and parallel to the axis.
Particle moves along the axis, so velocity $\vec{v}$ is parallel to $\vec{B}$.
Magnetic force $\vec{F} = Q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) = 0$ (since $\vec{v} \parallel \vec{B}$)
Only gravity acts on the particle.
Acceleration $a = g$

subject: Physics
solution:
For parallel combination: $k_{eq} = k_1 + k_2 = 10 + 20 = 30$ N/m
For sum, absolute errors add:
$\Delta k_{eq} = \Delta k_1 + \Delta k_2 = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5$ N/m
Percentage error $= \frac{\Delta k_{eq}}{k_{eq}} \times 100 = \frac{0.5}{30} \times 100 = 1.67\%$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given: $h = 5$ cm, $A_1 = 6$ cm², $A_2 = 3$ cm², $g = 1000$ cm/s²
By continuity: $A_1v_1 = A_2v_2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2v_1$
By Bernoulli's equation: $P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$
Since $P_1 - P_2 = \rho gh$:
$\rho gh = \frac{1}{2}\rho(4v_1^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{3\rho v_1^2}{2}$
$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 5}{3}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}$ cm/s
Rate of flow $Q = A_1v_1 = 6 \times \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3}$ cm³/s

subject: Physics
solution:
For beam expander using two convex lenses:
Magnification $= \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{f_2}{f_1}$
$\frac{14}{2} = \frac{f_2}{40}$
$f_2 = 7 \times 40 = 280$ mm

subject: Physics
solution:
For microscope at normal adjustment:
$M = \frac{L}{f_o} \times \frac{D}{f_e}$
where $L = 32$ cm, $f_o = 2$ cm, $f_e = 4$ cm, $D = 25$ cm
$M = \frac{32}{2} \times \frac{25}{4} = 16 \times 6.25 = 100$

subject: Physics
solution:
When string at B is cut, rod rotates about A.
Torque about A: $\tau = mg \times \frac{l}{2}$
Moment of inertia about A: $I_A = \frac{ml^2}{3}$
Angular acceleration: $\alpha = \frac{mg \cdot l/2}{ml^2/3} = \frac{3g}{2l}$
Acceleration of COM: $a_{cm} = \alpha \times \frac{l}{2} = \frac{3g}{4}$
Newton's second law: $mg - T = ma_{cm}$
$T = mg - \frac{3mg}{4} = \frac{mg}{4}$

subject: Physics
solution:
For vertical rod with axis at end P: $I_1 = \frac{ML^2}{3}$
For horizontal rod centered at distance L from P:
$I_2 = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{M}{L}(x^2 + L^2)dx = \frac{M}{L}\left[\frac{x^3}{3} + L^2x\right]_{-L/2}^{L/2}$
$= \frac{M}{L}\left[\frac{L^3}{12} + L^3\right] = \frac{13ML^2}{12}$
Total: $I = \frac{ML^2}{3} + \frac{13ML^2}{12} = \frac{4ML^2 + 13ML^2}{12} = \frac{17ML^2}{12}$
$x = 17$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the inputs be A and B. The circuit is composed of NOR gates.
A NOR gate with its inputs tied together acts as a NOT gate.
The output of the first NOR gate (input A) is $Y_1 = \overline{A+A} = \bar{A}$.
The output of the second NOR gate (input B) is $Y_2 = \overline{B+B} = \bar{B}$.
These outputs are fed into a third NOR gate. Its output is $Y_3 = \overline{Y_1 + Y_2} = \overline{\bar{A} + \bar{B}}$.
Using De Morgan's theorem, $Y_3 = (\overline{\bar{A}}) \cdot (\overline{\bar{B}}) = A \cdot B$.
This output $Y_3$ is the input to the fourth NOR gate, which is configured as a NOT gate.
The final output is $Y_{out} = \overline{Y_3} = \overline{A \cdot B}$.
The expression $\overline{A \cdot B}$ represents a NAND gate.

subject: Physics
solution:
Total length at 30°C = 120 cm, each rod = 60 cm
$\alpha_{Al} = 24 \times 10^{-6}$/°C, $\alpha_{Steel} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$/°C
$\Delta T = 100 - 30 = 70$°C
$\Delta L_{Al} = 60 \times 24 \times 10^{-6} \times 70 = 0.1008$ cm
$\Delta L_{Steel} = 60 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 70 = 0.0504$ cm
Total expansion $= 0.1008 + 0.0504 = 0.1512$ cm
Final length $= 120 + 0.1512 = 120.15$ cm

subject: Physics
solution:
Flow rate = 5.0 L/min = 5.0 kg/min, $\Delta T = 87 - 27 = 60$°C
Heat required per minute: $Q = mc\Delta T = 5.0 \times 4200 \times 60 = 1.26 \times 10^6$ J/min
If m grams of gas consumed per minute:
$m \times 5.0 \times 10^4 = 1.26 \times 10^6$
$m = \frac{1.26 \times 10^6}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 25.2$ g/min
Rate $= \frac{25.2}{60} = 0.42$ g/s

subject: Physics
solution:
Given: $n = 10$ mol,
$\Delta T = 40 - 30 = 10°C$,
$C_P = 7$ cal/mol·°C,
$R = 2$ cal/mol·°C
For ideal gas: $C_V = C_P - R = 7 - 2 = 5$ cal/mol·°C
Change in internal energy: $\Delta U = nC_V\Delta T = 10 \times 5 \times 10 = 500$ cal

subject: Physics
solution:
All terms must have dimension of pressure $[ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$.
For $(h + Bt)$: Since $\rho gh$ has dimension of pressure, $[h] = [L]$.
For dimensional consistency: $[Bt] = [L]$, so $[B] = [LT^{-1}] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$
For $\frac{A}{Bt^2}$: $\frac{[A]}{[B][T^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$
$[A] = [B][T^2][ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [LT^{-1}][T^2][ML^{-1}T^{-2}] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$
$[A] = [ML^0T^{-1}]$ and $[B] = [M^0LT^{-1}]$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given: $d = 0.1$ cm, $D = 50$ cm, $n = 1.5$, Shift $= 0.2$ cm
Optical path difference by sheet: $\Delta = (n-1)t$
Shift of central fringe: Shift $= \frac{(n-1)t \times D}{d}$
$0.2 = \frac{(1.5-1) \times t \times 50}{0.1} = \frac{0.5 \times 50 \times t}{0.1} = 250t$
$t = \frac{0.2}{250} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ cm

subject: Physics
solution:
Wave velocity in string: $v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$
Time to reach joint: $t = \frac{L}{v} = L\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{T}}$
$t_1 = L_A\sqrt{\frac{\mu_A}{T}}$ and $t_2 = L_B\sqrt{\frac{\mu_B}{T}}$
$\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{L_A}{L_B} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}} = \frac{2.5}{1.5} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}$
$= \frac{5}{3} \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{5}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 1.18$

subject: Physics
solution:
Force is related to potential energy by $F = -\frac{dV}{dx}$.
Magnitude of force $|F| = \left|\frac{dV}{dx}\right|$ = |slope of V-x graph|
From graph coordinates: A(1, 0), B(1.5, 1), C(2, 4), D(4, 4), E(5, 3)
$|F_{AB}| = \frac{1-0}{1.5-1} = 2$
$|F_{BC}| = \frac{4-1}{2-1.5} = 6$
$|F_{CD}| = \frac{4-4}{4-2} = 0$
$|F_{DE}| = \frac{|3-4|}{5-4} = 1$
Ranking: $F_{BC} > F_{AB} > F_{DE} > F_{CD}$
